g.data-narration: Welcome to Memory Allocation - a fundamental concept in operating systems that manages RAM efficiently.
Memory allocation assigns RAM to processes, managing this finite resource for concurrent operations efficiently.
g.text: Assignment of memory space to processes and data structures. The OS acts as
memory manager, tracking used/free memory, allocating and reclaiming as needed.

g.data-narration: Proper memory management impacts performance, throughput, and enables efficient multiprogramming for better CPU utilization.
Contiguous allocation stores each process in a single continuous block. Fixed partitioning divides memory into predetermined partitions.
g.text: Memory divided into fixed partitions at system startup. Each partition holds one process.

g.data-narration: Variable partitioning creates exact-sized partitions when processes arrive, eliminating internal fragmentation but requiring sophisticated allocation algorithms.
g.text: No pre-partitioning. Partitions created on-demand with exact size. Eliminates internal fragmentation.
Processes loaded contiguously • Holes (free space) between processes

g.data-narration: Four algorithms allocate memory: First Fit (fastest), Best Fit (smallest hole), Worst Fit (largest hole), Next Fit (continues searching).
Internal fragmentation wastes space within allocated blocks. External fragmentation splits free memory into non-contiguous pieces.
g.text: Total free ≥ request but not contiguous

g.data-narration: Paging divides memory into fixed-size frames and pages, eliminating external fragmentation through non-contiguous allocation.
g.text: Eliminates external fragmentation by dividing both physical memory and logical address space into fixed-size blocks.

g.data-narration: Virtual address divides into page number and offset. Page table maps to physical frame, combining with offset gives physical address.
Segmentation divides memory into logical segments like code, data, stack based on program structure with variable sizes.
g.text: Divides process address space into logical segments: code, data, stack, heap. Each segment has base and limit.

g.data-narration: Segmentation uses segment table with base and limit. Address validates offset against limit, then adds base for physical address.
TLB caches recent page translations for faster access. Effective Access Time (EAT) calculates average memory access considering TLB hits.
g.text: TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) is a hardware cache storing recent page table entries for fast translation.
p = hit ratio, τ = access time

g.data-narration: Modern systems combine both: segments divide logical space, pages divide segments - combining logical organization with efficient physical management.
g.text: Modern OS use both: Logical address → Segmented → Paginated → Physical. Combines advantages of both approaches.

g.data-narration: Key formulas to remember: Physical address, page number, offset calculations, and the 50-percent rule for external fragmentation.
g.text: ~50% of memory in First Fit systems

g.data-narration: Remember: paging eliminates external but introduces internal fragmentation. First fit is faster, best fit minimizes waste. TLB speeds up address translation.
g.text: • Internal: within allocated blocks | External: between free blocks
• First Fit: fast, more fragmentation | Best Fit: slow, less waste
• Paging: no external, has internal | Segmentation: logical view + protection
• Page table size = 2^(addr bits - offset bits) × entry size
